mn40:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Aṅgans at a town of the Aṅgans named Assapura.
mn40:10-12.1: With a mind imbued with compassion [*karuṇā*] …
mn40:10-12.2: with a mind imbued with gladness [*muditā*] …
mn40:10-12.3: with a mind imbued with equanimity [*upekkhā*], he dwells pervading one direction, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, and across, everywhere and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind imbued with equanimity [*upekkhā*], abundant, expansive, measureless, without hostility, without ill will.
mn40:13.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a lotus pond with clear water, sweet water, cool water, with clean banks, delightful.
mn40:13.2: And a person might come from the eastern direction, scorched by the heat, overwhelmed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched.
mn40:13.3: Having come to that lotus pond, he would remove his thirst for water and relieve his oppression from the heat … Or a person might come from the western direction … Or a person might come from the northern direction … Or a person might come from the southern direction. From whatever direction a person might come, scorched by the heat, overwhelmed by the heat, weary, thirsty, parched, having come to that lotus pond, he would remove his thirst for water and relieve his oppression from the heat.
mn40:13.4: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, if one has gone forth from the home life into homelessness from a khattiya family, and having approached the *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*] proclaimed by the Tathāgata, develops goodwill [*mettā*], compassion [*karuṇā*], gladness [*muditā*], and equanimity [*upekkhā*] in this way, he attains inward stilling. Because of inward stilling, I say that he ‘has undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic.’ If from a brahmin family … If from a merchant family … If from a worker family … If from whatever family one has gone forth from the home life into homelessness, and having approached the *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*] proclaimed by the Tathāgata, develops goodwill [*mettā*], compassion [*karuṇā*], gladness [*muditā*], and equanimity [*upekkhā*] in this way, he attains inward stilling.
mn40:13.5: Because of inward stilling, I say that he ‘has undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic.’
mn40:14.1: If one has gone forth from the home life into homelessness from a khattiya family,
mn40:14.2: and through the destruction of the *āsavas* dwells, having directly known and realized for himself in this very life the liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] without *āsavas* [*anāsavaṁ*], having attained them,
mn40:14.3: through the destruction of the *āsavas* he is an ascetic. If from a brahmin family … If from a merchant family … If from a worker family … If from whatever family one has gone forth from the home life into homelessness, and through the destruction of the *āsavas* dwells, having directly known and realized for himself in this very life the liberation of mind [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] without *āsavas* [*anāsavaṁ*], having attained them—through the destruction of the *āsavas* he is an ascetic.”
mn40:14.5: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn40:14.6: The Shorter Discourse at Assapura is finished, the tenth.
mn40:14.7: The fourth chapter, the Greater Pairings Chapter, is finished.
mn40:15.1: Giñjaka, the Sāla Grove, and Avoidance,
mn40:15.2: then Saccaka’s refutation for the discerning one;
mn40:15.4: with Kevaṭṭa, Assapura, and the Matted-Hair Ascetic.
mn40:2.2: And when you are asked, ‘Who are you?’ you claim, ‘We are ascetics.’
mn40:2.3: Since you are thus designated and thus claim:
mn40:2.4: ‘We will practice the way of practice proper to an ascetic.
mn40:2.5: In that way, this designation of ours will be true, and this claim of ours will be genuine.
mn40:2.6: And for those whose robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and provisions for the sick we make use of, those acts toward us will become very fruitful and of great benefit. And this going forth of ours will not be barren, but fruitful and fertile.’
mn40:2.7: That is how you should train yourselves, *bhikkhus*.
mn40:3.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, has a *bhikkhu* not undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic?
mn40:3.2: Any *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, who is covetous, whose covetousness has not been abandoned; whose mind is ill-disposed, whose ill will has not been abandoned; who is angry, whose anger has not been abandoned; who is resentful, whose resentment has not been abandoned; who is denigrating, whose denigration has not been abandoned; who is insolent, whose insolence has not been abandoned; who is envious, whose envy has not been abandoned; who is stingy, whose stinginess has not been abandoned; who is deceitful, whose deceit has not been abandoned; who is deceptive, whose deception has not been abandoned; who has bad desires, whose bad desire has not been abandoned; who has wrong view, whose wrong view has not been abandoned—
mn40:3.3: because of the non-abandoning of these stains of an ascetic, these faults of an ascetic, these blemishes of an ascetic, these states leading to ruin and to be felt in a bad destination, I say that he ‘has not undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic.’
mn40:4.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, there were a kind of weapon called a mataja, double-edged, well-polished and sharp.
mn40:4.2: And it were wrapped and bundled up in a patchwork robe.
mn40:4.3: That is the simile I give, *bhikkhus*, for the going forth of this *bhikkhu*.
mn40:5.1: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he wears the outer robe.
mn40:5.10: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he wears matted hair.
mn40:5.2: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he goes naked.
mn40:5.3: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he is smeared with dust and dirt.
mn40:5.4: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he plunges into water.
mn40:5.5: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he lives at the root of a tree.
mn40:5.6: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he lives in the open air.
mn40:5.7: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he remains standing.
mn40:5.8: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he eats at intervals.
mn40:5.9: I do not call someone an ascetic, *bhikkhus*, merely because he recites chants.
mn40:6.1: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who wears the outer robe, covetousness were abandoned merely by wearing the outer robe; ill will were abandoned for one whose mind is ill-disposed; anger were abandoned for one who is angry; resentment were abandoned for one who is resentful; denigration were abandoned for one who is denigrating; insolence were abandoned for one who is insolent; envy were abandoned for one who is envious; stinginess were abandoned for one who is stingy; deceit were abandoned for one who is deceitful; deception were abandoned for one who is deceptive; bad desire were abandoned for one who has bad desires; wrong view were abandoned for one who has wrong view—then his friends and companions, relatives and blood-kin, would make him a wearer of the outer robe from the moment he was born and would urge him only to be a wearer of the outer robe:
mn40:6.10: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who eats at intervals …
mn40:6.11: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who recites chants …
mn40:6.12: If, *bhikkhus*, for one with matted hair, covetousness were abandoned merely by wearing matted hair; ill will were abandoned for one whose mind is ill-disposed; anger were abandoned for one who is angry; resentment were abandoned for one who is resentful; denigration were abandoned for one who is denigrating; insolence were abandoned for one who is insolent; envy were abandoned for one who is envious; stinginess were abandoned for one who is stingy; deceit were abandoned for one who is deceitful; deception were abandoned for one who is deceptive; bad desire were abandoned for one who has bad desires; wrong view were abandoned for one who has wrong view—then his friends and companions, relatives and blood-kin, would make him matted-haired from the moment he was born and would urge him only to be matted-haired:
mn40:6.13: ‘Come, good man, be matted-haired. For when you are matted-haired, covetousness will be abandoned for you merely by wearing matted hair; ill will will be abandoned for one whose mind is ill-disposed; anger will be abandoned for one who is angry … bad desire will be abandoned for one who has bad desires; wrong view will be abandoned for one who has wrong view.’
mn40:6.14: But because I see some person here, *bhikkhus*, who even though matted-haired is covetous, ill-disposed in mind, angry, resentful, denigrating, insolent, envious, stingy, deceitful, deceptive, with bad desires, and with wrong view, therefore I do not call someone an ascetic merely because he wears matted hair.
mn40:6.2: ‘Come, good man, be one who wears the outer robe. For when you wear the outer robe, covetousness will be abandoned for you merely by wearing the outer robe; ill will will be abandoned for one whose mind is ill-disposed; anger will be abandoned for one who is angry; resentment will be abandoned for one who is resentful; denigration will be abandoned for one who is denigrating; insolence will be abandoned for one who is insolent; envy will be abandoned for one who is envious; stinginess will be abandoned for one who is stingy; deceit will be abandoned for one who is deceitful; deception will be abandoned for one who is deceptive; bad desire will be abandoned for one who has bad desires; wrong view will be abandoned for one who has wrong view.’
mn40:6.3: But because I see some person here, *bhikkhus*, who even though he wears the outer robe is covetous, ill-disposed in mind, angry, resentful, denigrating, insolent, envious, stingy, deceitful, deceptive, with bad desires, and with wrong view, therefore I do not call someone an ascetic merely because he wears the outer robe.
mn40:6.4: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who goes naked …
mn40:6.5: If, *bhikkhus*, for one smeared with dust and dirt …
mn40:6.6: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who plunges into water …
mn40:6.7: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who lives at the root of a tree …
mn40:6.8: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who lives in the open air …
mn40:6.9: If, *bhikkhus*, for one who remains standing …
mn40:7.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, has a *bhikkhu* undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic?
mn40:7.2: Any *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, who is covetous, but whose covetousness has been abandoned; whose mind is ill-disposed, but whose ill will has been abandoned; who is angry, but whose anger has been abandoned; who is resentful, but whose resentment has been abandoned; who is denigrating, but whose denigration has been abandoned; who is insolent, but whose insolence has been abandoned; who is envious, but whose envy has been abandoned; who is stingy, but whose stinginess has been abandoned; who is deceitful, but whose deceit has been abandoned; who is deceptive, but whose deception has been abandoned; who has bad desires, but whose bad desire has been abandoned; who has wrong view, but whose wrong view has been abandoned—
mn40:7.3: because of the abandoning of these stains of an ascetic, these faults of an ascetic, these blemishes of an ascetic, these states leading to ruin and to be felt in a bad destination, I say that he ‘has undertaken the way of practice proper to an ascetic.’
mn40:8.1: He regards himself as purified from all these bad, unskillful dhammas (…).
mn40:8.2: For one who regards himself as purified from all these bad, unskillful dhammas (…), joy arises; for one who is joyful, *pīti* arises; for one with *pīti* in the mind [*pītimanassa*], the body grows tranquil; one whose body is tranquil feels *sukha*; for one who is experiencing *sukha*, the mind settles in *samādhi*.
mn40:9.1: He dwells pervading one direction with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettā*], likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. So above, below, and across, everywhere and in every respect, he dwells pervading the whole world with a mind imbued with goodwill [*mettā*], abundant, expansive, measureless, without hostility, without ill will.
